title: - BYZANTINE-ROBUST LEARNING ON HETEROGE NEOUS DATASETS VIA RESAMPLING [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - BYZANTINE-ROBUST LEARNING ON HETEROGE NEOUS DATASETS VIA RESAMPLING

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

In Byzantine robust distributed optimization, a central server wants to train a machine learning model over data distributed across multiple workers. However, a fraction of these workers may deviate from the prescribed algorithm and send arbitrary messages to the server. While this problem has received significant attention recently, most current defenses assume that the workers have identical data. For realistic cases when the data across workers are heterogeneous (non-iid), we design new attacks which circumvent these defenses leading to significant loss of performance. We then propose a simple resampling scheme that adapts existing robust algorithms to heterogeneous datasets at a negligible computational cost. We theoretically and experimentally validate our approach, showing that combining resampling with existing robust algorithms is effective against challenging attacks.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Distributed or federated machine learning, where the data is distributed across multiple workers, has become an increasingly important learning paradigm both due to growing sizes of datasets, as well as privacy and security concerns. In such a setting, the workers collaborate to train a single model without transmitting their data directly over the networks (McMahan et al., 2016; Bonawitz et al., 2019; Kairouz et al., 2019). Due to the presence of either actively malicious agents in the network, or simply due to system and network failures, some workers may disobey the protocols and send arbitrary messages; such workers are also known as _Byzantine_ workers (Lamport et al., 2019). Byzantine robust optimization algorithms combine the gradients received by all workers using robust aggregation rules, to ensure that the training is not impacted by the malicious workers.

While this problem has received significant recent attention, (Alistarh et al., 2018; Blanchard et al., 2017; Yin et al., 2018a), most of the current approaches assume that the data present on each different worker has identical distribution. In this work, we show that existing Byzantine-robust methods catastrophically fail in the realistic setting when the data is distributed heterogeneously across the workers. We then propose a simple resampling scheme which can be readily combined with existing aggregation rules to allow robust training on heterogeneous data.

**Contribution.** Concretely, our contributions in this work are

- We show that when the data across workers is heterogeneous, existing robust rules might not converge, even without any Byzantine adversaries.

- We propose two new attacks, normalized gradient and mimic, which take advantage of data heterogeneity and circumvent median and sign-based defenses (Blanchard et al., 2017; Pillutla et al., 2019; Li et al., 2019).

- We propose a simple new resampling step which can be used before any existing robust aggregation rule. We instantiate our scheme with KRUM and theoretically prove that the resampling generalizes it to the setting of heterogeneous data.

- Our experiments evaluate the proposed resampling scheme against known and new attacks and show that it drastically improves the performance of 3 existing schemes on realistic heterogeneously distributed datasets.

**Setup and notations.** We study the general distributed optimization problem

If the aggregation rule is the arithmetic mean, then (2) recovers standard minibatch SGD.

**Byzantine attack model.** In each iteration, there is a set **Byz** of at most _f_ Byzantine workers. The remaining workers are **good** , thus follow the described protocol. A Byzantine worker _j ∈_ **Byz** can deviate from protocol and send an arbitrary vecter to the server. Besides, we also allow that Byzantine workers can collude with each other and know every state of the system. Unlike martingale-based approaches like (Alistarh et al., 2018), we allow the set **Byz** to change over time (Blanchard et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2017; Mhamdi et al., 2018).

## 2 RELATED WORK

There has been significant recent work of the case when the workers have identical data distributions (Blanchard et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2017; Mhamdi et al., 2018; Alistarh et al., 2018; Mhamdi et al., 2018; Yin et al., 2018a;b; Su & Xu, 2018; Damaskinos et al., 2019). We discuss the most pertinent of these methods next. Blanchard et al. (2017) formalize the Byzantine robust setup and propose a distance-based approach KRUM which selects a worker whose gradient is very close to at least half the other workers. A different approach involves using the median and its variants (Blanchard et al., 2017; Pillutla et al., 2019; Yin et al., 2018a). Yin et al. (2018a) propose to use and analyze the coordinate-wise median method (CM). Pillutla et al. (2019) use a smoothed version of Weiszfeld’s algorithm to iteratively compute an approximate geometric median of the input gradients. In a third approach, (Bernstein et al., 2018) propose to use the signs of the gradients and then aggregate them by majority vote, however, (Karimireddy et al., 2019) show that it may not always converge. Finally, Alistarh et al. (2018) use a martingale-based aggregation rule which gives a sample complexity optimal algorithm for iid data. The distance-based approach of KRUM was later extended in Mhamdi et al. (2018) who propose BULYAN to overcome the _dimensional leeway_ attack. This is the so called _strong Byzantine resilience_ and is orthogonal to the question of non-iid-ness we study here. Recently, (Peng & Ling, 2020; Yang & Bajwa, 2019a;b) studied Byzantine-resilient algorithms in the decentralized setting where there is no central server available. Extending our techniques to the decentralized setting is an important direction for future work.

In a different line of work, (Lai et al., 2016; Diakonikolas et al., 2019) develop sophisticated spectral techniques to robust estimate the mean of a high dimensional multi-variate standard Gaussian distribution where samples are evenly distributed in all directions and the attackers are concentrated in one direction. Very recent work (Data & Diggavi, 2020) extend the theoretical analysis to non-convex, strongly-convex and non-i.i.d setup under a gradient dissimilarity assumption and propose a gradient compression scheme on top of it. Our resampling trick can be combined with it to further reduce gradient dissimilarity.

Many attacks have been devised for distributed training. For the iid setting, the state-of-the-art attacks are (Baruch et al., 2019; Xie et al., 2019b). The latter attack is very strong when the fraction of adversaries is large (nearly half), but in this work we focus on settings when this fraction is quite small (e.g. _≤_ 0 _._ 2). Further our normalized mean attack Section 3.2 is inspired by (Xie et al., 2019b). The former work focuses on attacks which are coordinated across time steps. Developing strong practical defenses even in the iid case against such time-coordinated attacks remains an open problem. In this work, we sidestep this issue by restricting ourselves to new attacks made possible by non-iid data and studying how to overcome them. We focus on schemes which work in the iid setting, but fail with non-iid data. Once a new method which can defend against (Baruch et al., 2019) is developed, our proposed scheme shows how to adapt such a method to the important non-iid case. For the non-iid setting, backdoor attacks are designed to take advantage of heavy-tailed data and manipulate

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

model inference on specific subtask, rather than lower the overall accuracies of training (Bagdasaryan et al., 2018; Bhagoji et al., 2018). In contrast, this paper is not intended to address aforementioned challenges but rather to defend the attacks that lower the training accuracies in the non-iid setting.

As far as we are aware, only (Li et al., 2019; Ghosh et al., 2019; Sattler et al., 2020) explicitly investigate Byzantine robustness with non-iid workers. Li et al. (2019) proposes an SGD variant (RSA) which modifies the original objective by adding an _ℓ_ 1 penalty. Ghosh et al. (2019); Sattler et al. (2020) assume that all workers belong to an apriori fixed number of clusters and use an outlier-robust clustering method to recover these clusters. If we assume that the server has the entire training dataset and can control the distribution of samples to good workers, Xie et al. (2019a); Chen et al. (2018); Rajput et al. (2019) show that non-iid-ness can be overcome. Typical examples of this is distributed training of neural networks on public cloud, or volunteer computing Meeds et al. (2015); Miura & Harada (2015). However, none of these methods are applicable in the standard federated learning setup we consider here. We aim to minimize the original loss function over workers while respecting the non-iid data locality, i.e. the partition of the given heterogeneous dataset over the workers, without data transfer.

## 3 ATTACKS AGAINST EXISTING AGGREGATION SCHEMES

In this section we show that when the data across the workers is heterogeneous (non-iid), then we can design new attacks which take advantage of the heterogeneity, leading to the failure of existing aggregation schemes. We study three classes of robust aggregation schemes: i) schemes which select a representative worker in each round (e.g. KRUM (Blanchard et al., 2017)), ii) schemes which use normalized means (e.g. RSA (Li et al., 2019)), and iii) those which use the median (e.g. RFA (Pillutla et al., 2019)). We show realistic settings under which each of these classes would fail when faced with heterogeneous data.

- 3.1 FAILURE OF REPRESENTATIVE WORKER SCHEMES ON NON-IID DATA

Algorithms like KRUM select workers who are representative of a majority of the workers, by relying on statistics such as pairwise differences between the various worker updates. Let ( _**g**_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**g** n_ ) be the gradients by the workers, _f_ of which are Byzantine (e.g. _n ≥_ 2 _f_ + 3 for KRUM). For _i ̸_ = _j_ , let _i → j_ denote that _**g** j_ belongs to the _n − f −_ 2 closest vectors to _**g** i_ . Then KRUM is defined as follows

However, when the data across the workers is heterogeneous, there is no ‘representative’ worker. This is because each worker computes their local gradient over vastly different local data. Hence, for convergence it is important to not only select a good (non-Byzantine) worker, but also ensure that each of the good workers is selected with roughly equal frequency. Hence KRUM suffers a significant loss in performance with heterogeneous data, even when there are _no Byzantine workers_ .

For example, when KRUM is used for iid datasets without adversary ( _f_ =0, see left of Figure 1a), the test accuracy is close to simple average and the gap can be filled by MULTI-KRUM (Blanchard et al., 2017). The right plot of Figure 1a also shows that KRUM’s selection of gradients is biased towards certain nodes. When KRUM is applied to non-iid datasets (the middle of Figure 1a), KRUM performs poorly even without any attack. This is because KRUM mostly selects gradients from a few nodes whose distribution is closer to others (the right of Figure 1a). This is an example of how robust aggregation rules may fail on realistic non-iid datasets.

- 3.2 ATTACKS ON NORMALIZED AGGREGATION SCHEMES

Instead of simply averaging the gradients, some methods first normalize them and then average. This limits the influence of the Byzantine workers since they cannot output extremely large gradients, and hence is more robust. For example RFA (Pillutla et al., 2019) with _T_ =1 uses following aggregation rule: _[n]_

Other methods such as RSA (Li et al., 2019) or signum (Bernstein et al., 2018) normalize entries coordinate-wise before taking a majority vote i.e. update the server model _**x**_ 0 on server using local model _**x** i_ from node _i_ (not gradient) using

However, a Byzantine worker can still craft an “omniscient” attack to foil robust aggregations, using an approach similar to the negative sum for the arithmetic mean (Blanchard et al., 2017; Li et al., 2019):

On the right side of Figure 1b, we can see that this attack lowers the accuracy of RFA-T1 significantly, as the number of Byzantine workers increases. Comparing to its iid counterpart, the normalized mean attack is even more impactful in the non-iid setting.

3.3 ATTACKS ON MEDIAN-BASED SCHEMES

Geometric median and its variants are popular in robust learning research (Blanchard et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2017; Pillutla et al., 2019; Yin et al., 2018a; Mhamdi et al., 2018). Given gradients _{_ _**g**_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**g** n}_ , we use the estimator

If the vectors _{_ _**g**_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**g** n}_ are drawn independently from the same distribution, intuitively most of them would concentrate around their mean. Then, even if there are some Byzantine outputs, the median would ignore those as outliers and output a ‘central’ point close to the mean.

However, when _{_ _**g**_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**g** n}_ are gradients over heterogeneous data, they may be vastly different from each other and do not concentrate around the mean. In such a scenario, the median such as (7) can be even less robust than simply taking the mean. Suppose that worker 0 is Byzantine and the remaining workers _{_ 1 _, . . . ,_ 2 _n}_ are good, with a total of 2 _n_ + 1 workers. Now suppose that _gi_ = ( _−_ 1) _[i]_ for all the workers, with half the good workers having _−_ 1 and the other half +1. This means that the true mean is 0, however, the median estimator (7) will output 1.

**Mimic attack.** This motivates our _mimic_ attack in which all Byzantine workers collude and agree to always send gradients from the same worker. We define a specialized attack, called _mimic2_ , where half of the good workers have same datasets and send _**g**_ 1 while the rest good workers send _**g**_ 2; then all Byzantine workers send _**v**_ = _**g**_ 1 such that the geometric median of the gradients received by the server is always _**g**_ 1. Therefore, this attack breaks geometric-median-based robust aggregation rules, by leading them to wrong solutions. The left plot of Figure 1c shows the impact of the mimic2 attack. Test accuracies of CM and RFA both drop drastically to around 50%.

## 4 ROBUST AGGREGATION ON NON-IID DATA

In Section 3 we have demonstrated how existing robust aggregation rules can fail in realistic non-iid scenarios, with and without attackers (Sections 3.2 and 3.3 and Section 3.1 respectively). To overcome this problem, we propose a simple new resampling-based aggregation rule for training, shown in Algorithm 1. More specifically, we choose _s-resampling without replacement_ in Algorithm 2 where each gradient can be sampled at most _s_ times. The key property of our rule is that after resampling, the resulting set of averaged gradients _{_ _**g**_ ¯ _t_ : _t ∈_ [ _T_ ] _}_ are much more homogeneous (lower variance). Then these averaged gradients are fed to existing Byzantine robust aggregation schemes, such as KRUM, see Section 5. Given an existing aggregation rule AGGR, we denote by AGGR _◦_ Resampling the resulting new robust aggregation rule for non-iid input gradients.

In the following proposition, we list the desired properties of Algorithm 2

_n_ **Proposition I.** _Given a population {_ _**g** i_ : _i ∈_ [ _n_ ] _} ⊂_ R _[d] of mean_ _**µ**_ := _n_[1] � _i_ =1 _**[g]**[i][and][variance] n σ_[2] := _n_[1] � _i_ =1 _[∥]_ _**[g]**[i][ −]_ _**[µ]**[∥]_[2] _[, let][ {]_ _**[g]**_[¯] _[t]_[:] _[ t][ ∈]_[[] _[T]_[]] _[}][ be the output of Algorithm 2 on][ {]_ _**[g]**[i]_[:] _[ i][ ∈]_[[] _[n]_[]] _[}][.][Then] • If there are no Byzantine workers, then {_ _**g**_ ¯ _t_ : _t ∈_ [ _T_ ] _} are identically distributed_ E[¯ _**g** t_ ] = _**µ** , var_ (¯ _**g** t_ ) = _snn−−_ 11 _[σ]_[2] _∀ t ∈_ [ _T_ ] (8)
_Proof._ Since Algorithm 2 resamples _s_ gradients to estimate a population of _sn_ samples, we can use sampling theory (Middleton, 1988, Ch. Survey Sampling) to compute the sample mean
Since the gradients are sampled at most _s_ times, at most _sf_ out of the _T_ gradients are affected by a Byzantine worker. Its mean and variance can be calculated in the same way shown above.

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

**Remark 1.** _For s_ = 1 _, resampling simply becomes shuffling of the input elements, and var_ (¯ _**g** t_ ) = _σ_[2] _is unchanged. For s >_ 1 _, the resampling scheme reduces the heterogeneity (variance) by approximately_ 1 _/s. Thus, increasing s leads to the resulting resampled gradients being a better estimator of the population mean, thus improving training convergence speed. On the other hand, increasing s also increases the number of resampled gradients which can be affected by a Byzantine worker. In particular, if f workers are Byzantine, then up to fs resampled gradients can be incorrect, which has to be taken into account by the employed robust aggregation rule. In practice, we found that using a small value s_ = 2 _was already sufficient to overcome heterogeneity._
**Remark 2.** _A natural question to ask is what happens if we resample with replacement but do not limit on the number of replacements. We discuss this additional algorithm variant in Appendix C._

Note that the _{_ _**g**_ ¯ _t_ : _t ∈_ [ _T_ ] _}_ are identically distributed but not independent. This does not directly fit into the original assumptions of Byzantine robust algorithms like KRUM and hence the robustness has to be reproved for our more general setting.

## 5 CONVERGENCE ANALYSIS WITH KRUM

In this section, we analyze the convergence of SGD with robust aggregation on non-iid data. Since the definition of robustness and other conditions vary from paper to paper, it is not possible to give a uniform proof perfectly fit for all methods. For example, (Yin et al., 2018a) assumes the gradients have bounded variance and skewness whereas others like KRUM, RFA, BULYAN does not. Thus we only analyze KRUM for its simplicity and popularity, and show that analysis is only slightly different from the original version. For other algorithms, we show by experiments that resampling helps them achieve better performance on heterogeneous data, see Section 6.
Definition A generalizes the Byzantine resilience of (Blanchard et al., 2017, Definition 1) to the cases where we have non-iid data. Let _G_ be an estimator of the good gradients.

Figure 2: Combining resampling with existing aggregation rules on non-iid MNIST dataset. In all experiments, there are 8 good and _f_ Byzantine workers. For each aggregation we resample and average _s_ gradients for _T_ = _n_ times.

## 6 EXPERIMENTS

In this section, we demonstrate the effect of resampling on datasets distributed in a non-iid fashion. Throughout the section, we illustrate the challenge, attacks, and defense by an example of training an MLP on the MNIST dataset (LeCun et al., 1998). In Appendix D, we present the results of similar experiments on Fashion-MNIST (Xiao et al., 2017) and CIFAR-10 (Krizhevsky et al., 2009). The dataset is sorted by labels and sequentially divided into equal parts among good workers; Byzantine workers have access to the dataset on all good workers. Implementations are based on PyTorch (Paszke et al., 2019) and will be made publicly available.

## 6.1 RESAMPLING AGAINST THE ATTACKS ON NON-IID DATA

In Section 3 we have presented how heterogeneous data can lead to failure of existing robust aggregation rules. Here we apply our proposed resampling with _T_ = _n_ , _s_ =2 to the same aggregation rules, showing that resampling overcomes the described failures. Results are presented in Figure 2. In Figure 2a, we show that using resampling helps KRUM to achieve better test accuracy on noniid data. Since resampling KRUM with _s_ =2 actually averages 2 gradients, we compare it with MULTIKRUM with _m_ =2. The middle of Figure 2a shows that MULTIKRUM with _m_ =2 performs better than KRUM, but KRUM with resampling is even better which suggests the resampling step improves the performance on non-iid data. The selection histogram on the rightmost part of Figure 2a shows that after resampling, KRUM’s selection is much more evenly distributed between the good workers. In Figure 2b, we show that resampling fixes RFA with _T_ =1 and allows it to defend against the normalized mean attack. The resampling-based aggregation can almost reach same accuracy for both iid and non-iid setup. In Figure 2c, while mimic attack does not work for median-based rules in the iid setting, resampling still slightly improves the performance due to variance reduction. In the non-iid setting, resampling drastically improves the accuracy to the same level as the iid setting.

## 6.2 RESAMPLING AGAINST GENERAL BYZANTINE ATTACKS

In Figure 3, we present thorough experiments on non-iid data over 10 workers with 2 Byzantine workers. In each subfigure, we compare an aggregation rule with its variant with resampling. Three aggregation rules are compared: KRUM, CM, RFA. In particular, we compare to RFA with both T=1 (normalized mean) and T=8 (geometric median).

**----- End of picture text -----**

Figure 3: Test accuracies of KRUM, CM, RFA under 5 kinds of attacks (and without attack) on non-iid datasets. There are 10 workers and 2 of them are Byzantine according to each attack row. Columns show each aggregation rule applied without (red) and with resampling (blue). Dashed lines for comparison are showing the same method without any Byzantine workers ( _f_ =0). For RFA, T1, T8 refers to the number of inner iterations of Weiszfeld’s algorithm.
**Attacks.** 5 different kinds of attacks are applied (one per row in the figure): bitflipping, labelflipping, gaussian attack, as well as the mimic and mimic2 attacks.
- **Bitflipping** : A Byzantine worker flips the sign bits and sends _−∇f_ ( _**x**_ ) instead of _∇f_ ( _**x**_ ) because of problems like hardware failures etc.
- **Labelflipping** : The dataset on workers have corrupted labels. For the MNIST dataset, we let Byzantine workers transform labels by _T_ ( _y_ ) := 9 _− y_ .
- **Gaussian** : A Byzantine worker sends a Gaussian random vector of 0 mean and isotropic covariance matrix with standard deviation 200 (Xie et al., 2018).

- **mimic & mimic2** : Explained in Section 3.3.

From Figure 3 we can see that resampling improves the accuracy on most of the tasks. The final accuracies achieved vary with the aggregation rules we use. Notice that RFA-T1 is more robust to the mimic attack than RFA-T8 in Figure 3 because more inner iterations lead to better approximate geometric median and less robust to normalized mean attacks. The normalized mean attack has been addressed in Section 3.2.

- 7 CONCLUSION

In this paper, we initiated a study of robust distributed learning problem under realistic heterogeneous data. We showed that many existing Byzantine-robust aggregation rules fail under simple new attacks, or sometimes even without any Byzantine workers. As a solution, we propose a resampling scheme

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

which effectively adapts existing robust algorithms to heterogeneous datasets at a negligible computational cost. We believe robustness under heterogeneous conditions has been an overlooked direction of research thus far and hope to inspire more work on this topic. Extending to the decentralized setting, stronger Byzantine adversaries, as well as obtaining optimal algorithms are other challenging directions for future work.

9

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and timely problem: the failure of Byzantine-robust aggregation rules in heterogeneous (non-IID) data settings. The core idea—using a resampling layer to reduce variance across workers before aggregation—is intuition-based and simple to implement. However, the theoretical development contains a significant notation and derivation error in Proposition I regarding the variance of the resampled mean. Furthermore, while the 'mimic' attack is clever, the paper’s experimental claims regarding the 'normalized mean attack' are somewhat underspecified. The paper provides a good bridge between standard robust optimization and federated learning challenges, though the formal proofs require careful correction of the variance constants.

comments.title: Incorrect variance derivation in Proposition I
comments.quote: E[\bar{\mathbf{g}}_t] = \mathbf{\mu}, var(\bar{\mathbf{g}}_t) = \frac{s}{s} \frac{n-n}{n-1} \sigma^2 \forall t \in [T] (8)
comments.explanation: In Proposition I, the formula provided for the variance of the resampled mean $\bar{\mathbf{g}}_t$ is mathematically nonsensical. The equation as written (likely due to an OCR error or typo) reads $\frac{n-n}{n-1}$, which would equal zero. Even assuming the standard formula for sampling without replacement for a sample of size $s$ from a population of size $n$, the variance should be $\frac{\sigma^2}{s} (\frac{n-s}{n-1})$. The text in Remark 1 claims the variance is reduced by 'approximately $1/s$', but the formal statement in Eq (8) does not support this. This is a technical error in a foundational proposition of the paper.

comments.title: Inconsistency between Byzantine count $f$ and $sf$ in Theorem II
comments.quote: If $s > 1$ and $2sf + 3 \le n$, then KRUM $\circ Resampling$ is $(\alpha, sf)$-Byzantine resilient
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify that resampling can amplify the influence of a Byzantine worker because one malicious gradient can be sampled into multiple resampled averages (up to $s$ times). However, they state the requirement $2sf + 3 \le n$. Given that $T=n$ (as per Algorithm 2), if $sf$ resampled gradients are malicious, the standard KRUM requirement (which requires more than $2 \times \text{adversaries} + 2$ nodes) would imply $n \ge 2sf + 3$. While logically consistent with their 'amplification' logic, this condition is extremely restrictive. For $s=2$ and $f=0.2n$, this condition is impossible to satisfy. The paper fails to discuss the practical implication that resampling theoretically 'weakens' the breakdown point of the aggregator while 'strengthening' its convergence on non-IID data.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Normalized Mean' attack description
comments.quote: \mathbf{v} := - \frac{1}{f} \sum_{i \in good} \frac{\mathbf{g}_i}{\|\mathbf{g}_i\|} (6)
comments.explanation: The 'normalized mean attack' defined in Equation 6 is intended to foil RFA. However, the explanation of why this attack is particularly effective in the non-IID setting versus the IID setting is thin. In non-IID settings, $\|\mathbf{g}_i\|$ can vary significantly between workers. While the text claims the attack is 'more impactful' in non-IID settings, it doesn't formally explain if this is due to the shift in the mean or the increased variance. A careful reader might be confused as to whether the benefit of resampling here is strictly variance reduction or if it changes the geometry of the attack itself.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency for sample size $s$ in Algorithm 2
comments.quote: If $c[j_i] == \bar{s}$ Break; $s$ Compute average $\bar{\mathbf{g}}_t := \frac{1}{s} \sum_{i=1}^s \mathbf{g}_{j_i}$
comments.explanation: Algorithm 2 contains several notation artifacts (likely OCR/typesetting). It uses 's-replacement' in the title but describes it as 'without replacement' in the text. The variable $\bar{s}$ appears suddenly in the 'If' statement without definition, and the 'Break; s' is likely a stray character. While a practitioner could likely infer the 'sampling without replacement' logic, the pseudocode is currently not reproducible as written.

comments.title: Unjustified assumption of $\alpha$ in Theorem II
comments.explanation: In Theorem II, the authors define $\alpha$ by an equation involving $\eta(T, sf)$ and $\sigma(\mathbf{x})$. However, $\eta$ is never defined in the text or the theorem. In the original KRUM paper, $\eta$ relates to the combination of the number of workers and the expected distance between good gradients. By skipping the definition of $\eta$, the authors make the bound in Theorem II non-trivial to verify or compare with the original IID bound.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling and timely contribution by addressing Byzantine robustness in the realistic setting of heterogeneous (non-iid) data, a major limitation of prior work. The proposed resampling scheme is simple, general, and effective, with strong empirical validation across multiple aggregation rules and attacks. The theoretical analysis extends KRUM's convergence to the non-iid setting with resampling, and the experiments convincingly show performance gains. However, there are several technical issues in the theoretical justification and definitions—particularly in the variance derivation and the handling of non-independence in resampled gradients—that weaken the rigor of the convergence claims. While the core idea is sound and the empirical results are strong, the theoretical section requires careful revision to align with standard assumptions in stochastic optimization.

comments.title: Incorrect variance formula in Proposition I
comments.explanation: The stated variance formula in Proposition I appears to be incorrect. The expression 'snn−−11' is likely an OCR artifact for 's(n−1)/(n−1)' or similar, but even with correction, the expected variance after averaging s samples without replacement should follow standard sampling variance formulas. For sampling without replacement and averaging s gradients from n, the variance of the average should be (n - s)/(s(n - 1)) * σ² when the population variance is σ². The authors claim a reduction by approximately 1/s (Remark 1), which is only accurate when n >> s and the finite population correction is negligible. However, their formula suggests a dependence on n that is not standard, and no derivation justifies it. The reference to 'sampling theory (Middleton, 1988)' is too vague to validate the result. This is a non-trivial derivation because the resampling is constrained (each gradient sampled at most s times), and the variance under this constraint is not standard. The incorrect expression undermines the theoretical foundation for the variance reduction claim.

comments.title: Mismatch between resampling algorithm and theoretical assumption of identical distribution
comments.quote: • If there are no Byzantine workers, then {_ _**g**_ ¯ _t_ : _t ∈_ [ _T_ ] _} are identically distributed_ E[¯ _**g** t_ ] = _**µ** , var_ (¯ _**g** t_ ) = ...
comments.explanation: The claim that the resampled averages {**ḡ**_t} are identically distributed is questionable given Algorithm 2. The algorithm samples gradients with a cap on how many times each original gradient can be used (c[j_i] < s), and T is set to n. This creates dependence on the order of sampling and imposes a hard constraint on reuse. As a result, the distribution of **ḡ**_t depends on t—early samples are drawn from the full pool, while later samples are drawn from a more limited set as some gradients hit their reuse limit. This induces non-stationarity and dependence across the **ḡ**_t, contradicting the i.i.d. or even identically distributed assumption used in the convergence analysis. While the expected value may still be µ due to symmetry, the distributions are not identical across t under this constrained resampling. This gap affects the validity of treating the resampled gradients as exchangeable inputs to KRUM.

comments.title: Unjustified independence assumption in convergence proof
comments.quote: Then we can conclude the almost sure convergence similar to (Blanchard et al., 2017, Proposition 2)
comments.explanation: The convergence analysis cites Blanchard et al. (2017), which assumes independent gradients across iterations. However, the paper explicitly notes that the resampled gradients {**ḡ**_t} are not independent—'Note that the {_ _**g**_ ¯ _t_ : _t ∈_ [ _T_ ] _}_ are identically distributed but not independent.' Despite this, the proof is deferred to an appendix and no modification to the martingale or independence assumptions is discussed. In SGD analysis, almost sure convergence typically relies on the gradient noise forming a martingale difference sequence, which requires independence or conditional independence given the past. The dependence in the resampled gradients violates this, and without a corrected analysis (e.g., using dependent convergence theorems), the conclusion does not follow. This is a critical gap in the theoretical justification, especially for a result claimed to establish convergence almost surely.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Definition A regarding random worker selection
comments.quote: Let G = G( **ξ** ) be an independent random variable which randomly selects a good worker i and samples a vector from Di and E G = **g**.
comments.explanation: The definition of G is ambiguous: it says 'randomly selects a good worker i' but does not specify the distribution. Uniform over good workers? Proportional to dataset size? This matters because in non-iid settings, the overall gradient mean is a weighted average. Later, the theorem assumes E[j,ξ][G_j(x,ξ)] = ∇L(x), which implies that the selection must be uniform or appropriately weighted to match the global gradient. But without clarifying the distribution of j, the definition is underspecified. Given the context, it's likely the authors intend uniform selection, but this should be explicit to avoid misinterpretation, especially since non-uniform sampling would bias the estimator.

comments.title: Missing factor in Byzantine budget adjustment in Theorem II
comments.quote: then KRUM ◦Resampling is (α, sf)-Byzantine resilient
comments.explanation: The theorem states that the resampling scheme makes KRUM (α, sf)-Byzantine resilient. This suggests that the effective number of Byzantine inputs is sf, which makes sense because each Byzantine worker’s gradient can appear in up to s resampled averages. However, KRUM requires n ≥ 2f + 3 for f Byzantines. Here, after resampling, there are T = n resampled gradients, and up to sf of them can be influenced. For KRUM to run safely on T inputs with sf Byzantines, we need T ≥ 2(sf) + 3, i.e., n ≥ 2sf + 3, which the theorem correctly states. But in the aggregation, KRUM runs on T gradients, not n. The paper does not confirm that T ≥ 2(sf) + 3 holds—T is set to n, so the condition is fine—but the reasoning is not made explicit. While this is likely correct, the transition from n original workers to T resampled gradients with sf effective adversaries needs more clarity to ensure the reader can verify the resilience condition.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important problem in Byzantine-robust distributed learning with heterogeneous data, proposing novel attacks and a resampling-based defense. The empirical results are compelling and demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach. However, there are several technical issues that need attention: missing key formulas, an incorrect variance calculation in a theoretical proposition, and garbled conditions in the convergence theorem. These issues affect the paper's technical rigor and reproducibility.

comments.title: Missing RFA aggregation formula
comments.quote: For example RFA (Pillutla et al., 2019) with T=1 uses following aggregation rule: _[n]_
comments.explanation: The paper states that RFA with T=1 uses a specific aggregation rule but the actual formula is missing. The text only shows '_[n]_' which appears to be incomplete. This is a critical formula since the normalized mean attack in Section 3.2 is designed specifically to target this aggregation rule. Without seeing the formula, readers cannot fully understand how the attack works or verify the claims.

comments.title: Missing geometric median formula
comments.quote: Given gradients {_ _**g**_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**g** n}_ , we use the estimator
comments.explanation: The paper introduces the geometric median estimator but doesn't actually show the formula. The text mentions equation (7) but it's not defined in the visible portion. This is important because the mimic attack is designed to exploit properties of the geometric median. Without the formula, readers cannot understand the attack mechanism or verify the theoretical claims.

comments.title: Incorrect variance formula in Proposition I
comments.explanation: The variance formula after resampling appears incorrect. For sampling s gradients without replacement from n gradients, the variance of the sample mean should be σ²/s × (n-s)/(n-1). The paper's formula σ² × (n-1)/(s×n) doesn't match standard sampling theory results. For example, with s=2 and n=10, the correct formula gives approximately 0.444σ² while the paper's gives 0.45σ². This error affects the theoretical foundation of the resampling scheme's variance reduction claim.

comments.title: Garbled conditions in Theorem II
comments.explanation: The conditions in Theorem II appear garbled and difficult to parse. There seem to be multiple conditions mixed together with unclear notation. This makes it impossible to verify the convergence theorem's requirements or understand how they relate to the standard assumptions in Byzantine-robust learning. The clarity of these conditions is crucial for the theoretical contribution.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper addresses a practically significant problem in federated learning: the susceptibility of robust aggregation rules to data heterogeneity (non-IID distribution) and the potential for new types of adversarial attacks this heterogeneity facilitates. The proposed resampling-based preprocessing is simple, intuitive, and experimentally grounded. However, there are significant notation issues and a lack of formal rigor in the presentation of the convergence proof, particularly around the assumptions and the definitions of the resampling process, which complicates assessing the theoretical claims.

comments.title: Notation confusion in Algorithm 2
comments.quote:
Input: { g_i : i ∈ [ n ] }, T := n , s , {c [ i ] := 0 : i ∈ [ n ] } 
 for t := 1 , . . . , T do 
 for i := 1 , . . . , s do 
 while Select j_i ∼ Uniform ([ n ]) do
comments.explanation: In the nested loop, 'i' is used as the index for both the 'for' loop (running from 1 to s) and the index 'ji' of the selected gradient. Furthermore, outside the inner loop, it creates an ambiguity regarding whether the same 'i' is meant to be the index in the counter array 'c[ji]'. While the logic is arguably clear enough to guess, the formal notation is broken as it creates a collision between iterator and selector.

comments.title: Inconsistency between algorithm description and pseudocode
comments.quote: If c_ [ j_i ] < s Then c_ [ j_i ] += 1 If c_ [ j_i ] == s Break; s Compute average g_ t := 1/s * sum_{i=1}^s g_{j_i}
comments.explanation: The algorithm specifies 's-replacement' where each gradient can be sampled at most 's' times. However, the condition 'If c_ [ j_i ] == s' checks whether the count reaches 's'. If the loop breaks as soon as a single gradient reaches count 's', the inner loop will not always iterate 's' times (for the variable 'i' from 1 to s). Consequently, setting 'g_t := 1/s * sum_{i=1}^s g_{j_i}' uses 's' in the denominator, but if the inner loop broke early, the sum may contain fewer than 's' terms, leading to a division by 's' that does not match the actual number of elements collected.

comments.title: Missing definition of epsilon in Theorem II
comments.quote: that for all x we have η ( T, sf ) · ∥ x ∥ d · σ ≥ ε > 0 and α 0 ≤ β < π/ 2 − α such that
comments.explanation: In Theorem II, the text refers to '\epsilon > 0' as a constant in a condition, but this constant appears to govern the boundary condition for convergence behavior ('beyond a certain horizon'). However, it is never defined what this epsilon represents relative to the loss function or optimization parameters, and the prose jumps into a condition involving 'η(T, sf)' which is not defined in the paper. This makes the non-trivial result impossible to verify.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important gap—Byzantine robustness under heterogeneous data—and proposes a simple resampling scheme that can be combined with existing robust aggregation rules. The motivation and high-level approach are clear, and the experiments demonstrate practical benefits. However, the technical presentation suffers from several OCR-related garbles, incomplete definitions, and one likely formula error in the variance calculation. These issues hinder reproducibility and make the theoretical claims hard to verify. The paper would benefit from a thorough cleanup of mathematical notation and completion of missing definitions.

comments.title: Garbled variance formula in Proposition I
comments.quote: var(¯g_t) = s n-1 n-s σ^2 ∀ t ∈ [T]
comments.explanation: The variance formula is presented as 's n-1 n-s σ^2', which is not a standard expression. From the context of finite-population sampling without replacement, the correct variance should be (n-s) σ^2 / (s (n-1)). The line appears to be an OCR corruption of a fraction. As written, it is ambiguous and could mislead a reader trying to compute or verify the variance reduction. The surrounding proof sketch mentions sampling theory, reinforcing that this is a typesetting error.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about population size in Proposition I proof
comments.quote: Since Algorithm 2 resamples s gradients to estimate a population of sn samples
comments.explanation: The proposition applies to a population of n gradients (one per worker), not sn. The Algorithm takes n input gradients and resamples from them without replacement to form averages. The factor 'sn' appears nowhere else and suggests confusion between the number of resampled averages (T = n) and the number of items per average (s). This does not affect the final variance expression (which the garbled formula is intended to represent), but as a standalone statement it is factually wrong and could mislead a reader about the sampling procedure.

comments.title: Missing definition of KRUM in Section 3.1
comments.quote: Then KRUM is defined as follows
comments.explanation: The sentence ends without any equation or formal description of KRUM. The paper relies on the reader knowing the definition from Blanchard et al. (2017), but a self-contained paper should either restate the rule or at least refer to an equation number. This omission makes the subsequent discussion of KRUM's behavior less concrete.

comments.title: Incomplete and garbled Definition A of Byzantine resilience
comments.explanation: The definition appears to have missing pieces: the symbol η is not introduced, T and sf have not been defined in this context, and the second inequality ends with a question mark (likely an OCR artifact). The definition is critical for the convergence theorem, but as presented it is too ambiguous to verify the proof. The intention (extending Blanchard et al.'s definition to the resampling setting) is clear, but the execution is incomplete.

comments.title: Garbled conditions in Theorem II
comments.quote: (iv) there exists a constant σ̃^2(x_0) such that for all x we have η(T,sf) · ∥x∥^2 · √d·σ(x) ≤ ∥∇L(x)∥· sin α and 0 ≤ α < π/2 such that ... (v) finally, beyond a certain horizon, there exist ε>0 and β < π/2 - α such that ∥∇L(x)∥ ≥ ε > 0 and ⟨x, ∇L(x)⟩ ≥ cos β∥x∥· ∥∇L(x)∥.
comments.explanation: The conditions are extremely fragmented. η(T,sf) is not defined, the inequality mixes norms and scalar products oddly, and the phrase 'beyond a certain horizon' is vague. This makes the theorem statement unverifiable. While the overall idea (KRUM+Resampling inherits Byzantine resilience with an adjusted number of Byzantine workers) is plausible, the technical details as presented cannot be parsed confidently.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of RSA/signum update in Section 3.2
comments.quote: Other methods such as RSA (Li et al., 2019) or signum (Bernstein et al., 2018) normalize entries coordinate-wise before taking a majority vote i.e. update the server model x_0 on server using local model x_i from node i (not gradient) using
comments.explanation: The text says the update uses local model x_i (not gradient), but signum and RSA typically operate on gradients or model differences, not directly on the models themselves. The subsequent equation is not shown, but the phrasing could confuse readers. If the authors intended to describe model averaging with sign-based aggregation, that is a different procedure from gradient-based signSGD. The ambiguity could lead to incorrect reproduction.

comments.title: Ambiguous break condition in Algorithm 2
comments.quote: If c[ji] == s Break; s
comments.explanation: The formatting of the break condition is unclear. It appears to say 'If c[ji] == s Break; s' with a trailing 's'. Likely the intended logic is to break out of the while loop when a gradient has been sampled s times, but the stray 's' at the end is confusing. The algorithm should specify where the break leads (the for-i loop or the while loop) and what happens to the accumulated count.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a novel resampling scheme to improve Byzantine-robust learning on heterogeneous datasets. The experiments convincingly demonstrate that existing robust aggregation methods fail on non-IID data and that the proposed resampling approach mitigates these failures. However, there are significant mathematical inconsistencies that undermine the theoretical foundation of the method. Specifically, the variance formula in Proposition I appears to have a typographical error that contradicts the sampling theory cited and the heuristic explanation provided in Remark 1. Additionally, a logical error in the illustrative example for the median attack in Section 3.3 compromises the clarity of the attack description. These issues must be corrected to validate the paper's claims.

comments.title: Variance formula in Proposition I contradicts sampling theory and heuristic
comments.explanation: In Proposition I, the authors derive the variance of the resampled gradients using sampling theory, stating that 'we can use sampling theory (Middleton, 1988, Ch. Survey Sampling) to compute the sample mean'. The formula provided, var(¯g_t) = ssn−−11 [σ]2, implies a factor of s/(sn-1). However, the standard formula for the variance of an unbiased sample mean of size k drawn without replacement from a finite population of size N is (N-k)/(N-1) * σ²/k. Here, N = sn and k = s. Substituting these values yields var(¯g_t) = (n-1)/(sn-1)σ². The authors' formula lacks the factor (n-1) in the numerator, which makes the variance depend on s (increasing variance as s increases) and contradicts Remark 1, which explicitly states the variance is reduced by approximately 1/s. This is a critical mathematical error.

comments.title: Logical inconsistency in Section 3.3 median example
comments.quote: Suppose that worker 0 is Byzantine and the remaining workers {1, ..., 2n} are good, with a total of 2n + 1 workers. Now suppose that gi = (-1)[i] for all the workers, with half the good workers having −1 and the other half +1. This means that the true mean is 0, however, the median estimator (7) will output 1.
comments.explanation: The authors use this example to demonstrate why the geometric median fails in heterogeneous settings. They posit that there is 1 Byzantine worker and 2n good workers. The good workers send alternating -1 and +1, and the Byzantine worker sends -1. The resulting sorted list contains (n+1) copies of -1 and n copies of +1. The median (the middle element) of this sorted list is -1, not +1. The author's conclusion that the median estimator 'will output 1' is mathematically incorrect based on the setup described. This makes the example confusing and fails to support the claim about the mimic attack's effectiveness against geometric median.

comments.title: Unused parameter in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: S, IS = Resampling({gi : i ∈ [n]}, f, T, s);
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 calls the Resampling function and explicitly passes the number of Byzantine workers 'f' as an argument. However, in Algorithm 2, the pseudocode for Resampling takes inputs {gi}, T, s, and a dictionary {ci}, but does not utilize the parameter f. While conceptually the server may know f for analysis, including it as an unused parameter in the algorithm signature is technically misleading and implies that the selection logic depends on f, which it does not.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation distinguishing gradients from models in Section 3.2
comments.quote: Other methods such as RSA (Li et al., 2019) or signum (Bernstein et al., 2018) normalize entries coordinate-wise before taking a majority vote i.e. update the server model x_0 on server using local model x_i from node i (not gradient) using ...
comments.explanation: The text claims to describe methods that aggregate 'gradients' (implied by the context of previous sections). However, the parenthetical remark '(not gradient)' and the phrase 'update the server model ... using local model x_i' suggests the description applies to the model parameters x rather than the gradient vectors g. Furthermore, the equation displayed immediately after describes an update on x_0, whereas RSA (Li et al., 2019) is defined in the literature as aggregating *normalized gradients*. This creates confusion regarding the exact mechanism of the normalized aggregation schemes being attacked.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper addresses a highly relevant and timely problem: the failure of Byzantine-robust aggregation rules under realistic non-iid data distributions. The motivation is clear, the proposed resampling strategy is intuitively sound, and the experimental results convincingly demonstrate its practical utility across multiple aggregation schemes. However, several critical mathematical expressions and algorithmic details appear garbled or underspecified in the provided text, likely due to OCR extraction or manuscript preparation issues. These gaps currently obstruct verification of the theoretical claims and reproduction of the proposed method. With careful correction of the missing formulas, clarified notation, and expanded algorithmic pseudocode, this work has strong potential for acceptance.

comments.title: Mangled Variance Formula in Proposition I
comments.quote: var_($\bar{\mathbf{g}}_t$) = $snn−−11[σ]2$ ∀ t ∈ [T] (8)
comments.explanation: The authors correctly aim to quantify how resampling reduces gradient heterogeneity, which is central to their theoretical contribution. Initially, I was concerned that the variance expression was nonsensical, but upon checking standard sampling theory, I recognize it as an OCR-corrupted or typographical error. For sampling $s$ items without replacement from a finite population of size $n$, the variance of the sample mean should scale with the finite population correction factor $\frac{n-s}{n-1}\frac{\sigma^2}{s}$. The current string does not match this standard form and prevents verification of the claim that variance decreases by approximately $1/s$. I recommend restating Equation (8) with the explicit finite-population correction term.

comments.title: Missing Mathematical Definition for the Normalized Gradient Attack
comments.quote: A Byzantine worker can still craft an “omniscient” attack to foil robust aggregations, using an approach similar to the negative sum for the arithmetic mean (Blanchard et al., 2017; Li et al., 2019):
comments.explanation: I initially expected the attack vector to be defined immediately after this sentence, as it is a named contribution designed to exploit data heterogeneity. The text jumps directly to discussing Figure 1b results without providing the actual gradient formulation. Without the explicit equation for the Byzantine worker's output, it is impossible to verify how the attack leverages the non-iid setting or to reproduce it. While I understand OCR may have dropped a formula, this is a core technical component. I suggest inserting the precise mathematical description of the Byzantine gradient vector.

comments.title: Unreadable Constraint in Theorem II Condition (iv)
comments.quote: (iv) there exists awhereconstant σ_[2] ( _**x**_ 0) := _≤ αsnn−−<_ 11 _[σ]˜ _[π/_[2][(] 2 _**[x]** such_[)] _[;
comments.explanation: The convergence analysis is a major strength of the paper, so Condition (iv) was crucial to verify. The string is severely garbled and appears to attempt bounding the learning rate or relating it to the gradient variance $\sigma^2$ and angular parameter $\alpha$. Because this condition directly feeds into the claim of $(\alpha, sf)$-Byzantine resilience and almost sure convergence, its exact form must be clear. I checked the surrounding text for context, but no alternative definition appears. This needs to be resolved from the source LaTeX before the theoretical result can be properly evaluated.

comments.title: Underspecified Sampling Logic in Algorithm 2
comments.quote: for i := 1, ..., s do while Select j_i ~ Uniform([n]) do if c[j_i] < s then c[j_i] += 1 If c[j_i] == s Break; s Compute average _**g**_ t :=[1] _s_  _i_ =1 _**[g]**[j] i_ Return
comments.explanation: The resampling mechanism is intended to limit each gradient's usage to at most $s$ times while drawing exactly $s$ indices per iteration. The pseudocode's control flow, however, is ambiguous: the `while Select ... do` loop lacks a termination condition for the inner sampling process, and it is unclear how indices are repeatedly drawn until counts are satisfied. Additionally, the stray `s Compute average` suggests a formatting or OCR error that obscures the averaging step. To ensure reproducibility, the algorithm should explicitly state the number of draws, how the index set is finalized, and how $\bar{\mathbf{g}}_t$ is constructed. A clearer procedural description or flowchart would resolve this.

comments.title: Contradictory Terminology in Section 4
comments.quote: More specifically, we choose _s-resampling without replacement_ in Algorithm 2 where each gradient can be sampled at most _s_ times.
comments.explanation: The phrase 's-resampling without replacement' is conceptually contradictory, as 'resampling' conventionally implies replacement, while 'without replacement' implies drawing unique items. I initially paused here, wondering if the authors meant $s$-fold resampling with replacement, or simply drawing $s$ unique gradients. Given that the variance reduction property hinges entirely on the sampling mechanism, this terminology could mislead readers about the statistical behavior of the aggregated gradients. I recommend adopting standard statistical phrasing such as 'sampling $s$ gradients without replacement' or '$s$-subset averaging' to align with the actual procedure and the variance analysis in Proposition I.
